content.text: Triage Cadence and Ownership Rules

A bug triage process without a defined cadence drifts into chaos: bugs accumulate, ownership is unclear, high-priority issues age in the backlog without progress, and engineers spend time re-triaging the same bug multiple times. A triage cadence anchors the process to fixed rhythms — daily for new incoming bugs and active high-severity items, weekly for priority backlog management, quarterly for icebox hygiene and trend analysis.

For GenAI platforms, cadence is more important than in traditional software because the bug landscape shifts more rapidly. A model update can introduce a new class of quality bugs overnight. A safety filter change can surface new edge cases. The triage cadence ensures these shifts are detected and responded to systematically rather than reactively.

attrs.alt: Bug triage cadence: daily, weekly, and quarterly cycles with owners and outputs

content.text: Daily Triage
Daily triage is the highest-frequency loop. Its purpose is to ensure that no bug sits unclassified for more than 24 hours and that active high-severity bugs are progressing.
Who Runs Daily Triage

Daily triage is owned by the L3 on-call engineer (or a designated triage rotation if L3 on-call is purely reactive). The triage owner has the authority to assign severity, priority, and owner without requiring approval — speed matters more than perfect consensus at this cadence. Disputed assignments are escalated to the weekly review, not debated in the daily.
Daily Triage Agenda

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'New bugs (filed since last triage)', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — assign initial severity and priority using the severity/priority matrix, assign an owner, and move from 'unclassified' to 'triaged'. Any bug that lacks enough information to classify is marked 'needs-info' with a deadline and a question sent to the reporter.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'S1 and S2 active bugs', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — brief status check: is the assigned owner actively working on this? Is there a blocker? If an S2 bug has had no update in 48 hours, escalate to the engineering manager.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Safety-flagged bugs', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — any bug with a safety flag is forwarded to the AI Safety team immediately, regardless of assigned priority. Do not wait for the daily meeting to surface safety bugs.

{'type': 'text', 'text': "'Needs-info' bugs past deadline", 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — if a reporter has not responded within the deadline, the bug is either closed (if low severity) or escalated to the customer account manager (if enterprise customer).
Daily Triage Time Budget
Daily triage should complete in 20–30 minutes. If it regularly runs longer, one of three things is wrong:
Bug volume is too high for a single engineer to review daily — add a second triage engineer or reduce noise by improving bug report requirements
Bugs lack enough information to classify quickly — improve the bug report template and enforce required fields
Triage is becoming a status meeting — status on active bugs should come from the tracking system, not from the triage meeting
Weekly Triage
The weekly triage meeting has a broader scope: it aligns the bug backlog with the sprint plan and manages the priority of P2 and P3 bugs relative to planned feature work.
Who Attends Weekly Triage

The weekly triage is run by the engineering manager and attended by the L2 support lead, L3 lead, product manager, and a representative from the AI Safety team (or the safety team's designated bug liaison). Account managers attend for the customer bug status portion.

The AI Safety team representative is a non-negotiable attendee. Safety-relevant bugs that have been in the backlog need a human safety expert confirming that the current mitigation is acceptable and the fix timeline is appropriate.
Weekly Triage Agenda in Detail
Agenda item
Time
Owner
Output
P1 and P2 bugs vs. sprint plan
15 min
Engineering manager
Confirm P1/P2 bugs are in current sprint; defer or escalate any that are not
P3 bugs: re-classification pass
10 min
Engineering manager + PM
Promote P3 bugs with high customer volume or age > 30 days to P2
Bug distribution trend
5 min
L3 lead
Flag any category where bug count increased > 20% week-over-week
Enterprise customer bug status
10 min
L2 support lead
Status update on all S2+ bugs affecting enterprise customers; ETAs for account managers
Safety bug status
5 min
Safety team rep
Confirm safety bugs are on track; approve any delay to safety-flagged fix timelines
New bugs that need discussion
5 min
All
Edge cases where initial severity/priority assignment needs team input
Priority Changes Require Documentation
When a bug's priority changes at the weekly meeting — whether promoted from P3 to P2 or demoted from P2 to P3 — the change must be logged in the bug ticket with:
The date of the change
Who made the decision
The rationale (why the change was made)

This documentation is critical when customers ask why a bug they reported as critical has not been fixed for six weeks. The triage decision trail provides a defensible explanation and shows that the bug was actively reviewed, not ignored.
Quarterly Triage Review

The quarterly review serves a different purpose from the daily and weekly cadences: it cleans up accumulated debt, draws conclusions from trends across the full quarter, and makes decisions about P4 (icebox) bugs.
P4 Icebox Decisions
The quarterly review is the designated time to make final decisions on P4 bugs:
P4 bug state
Decision
No customer impact in 90 days, no reproduction
Close as 'cannot reproduce' — leave a note that it should be reopened if seen again
Confirmed model limitation, no fix planned
Close as 'known limitation'; create or update KB article documenting the limitation and workaround
Engineering effort too high relative to impact
Close as 'wontfix'; document the decision rationale; inform account manager if enterprise customer affected
Circumstances have changed (new model, new feature)
Re-evaluate: what was P4 may now be P2 with the new context
Safety-related P4 bug
Never close as wontfix without Safety team sign-off — safety issues are not icebox material
Trend Analysis at the Quarterly Level
Quarterly reviews produce the bug quality trend report, which is shared with senior stakeholders. The key metrics reviewed:
Total bugs filed per quarter, trend vs. prior three quarters
S1/S2 bug rate as a percentage of total, trend
Safety-flagged bugs count, trend, and whether any safety events occurred
Bug backlog age distribution: what percentage of open bugs are > 30 days, > 90 days, > 180 days
P1 mean time to close, trend
Model version with the highest bug rate — are newer versions introducing more quality issues?
Ownership Rules

Triage cadence defines when things happen. Ownership rules define who is responsible for what. Without clear ownership, bugs with multiple potential owners become everyone's problem and no one's priority.
Owner Assignment at Daily Triage
Every bug must have a single named owner — not a team, not a group, not 'L2'. Ownership is assigned at the daily triage based on the bug's layer:
Bug category
Default owner
Escalation path
API / infrastructure error
L3 platform engineer on rotation
Platform lead if not resolved in 48h
Model quality issue (S1/S2)
ML platform engineer
ML team lead if not triaged in 24h
Model quality issue (S3/S4)
L2 engineer with ML skills
L3 if requires model-level investigation
SDK / client library bug
SDK team engineer
SDK lead if not triaged in 48h
Safety-related bug
AI Safety team (forwarded immediately)
Safety team lead; no delay acceptable
Documentation / KB gap
Technical writer or L2 engineer
L2 lead if not closed in 1 sprint
The Owner's Responsibilities
Being assigned as bug owner means:
Posting a status update in the ticket at least every 48 hours for P1/P2 bugs
Posting a status update at least every 7 days for P3 bugs
Identifying blockers and escalating proactively — do not wait for the weekly triage to surface a blocker
Writing a reproduction case if one does not already exist
Proposing a fix plan within the timeline set by priority (P1: within 1 day of assignment; P2: within 1 week)
Not reassigning the bug without explicit discussion — unilateral reassignments break ownership accountability
Ownership Reassignment
Bugs sometimes need to move between owners: an engineer goes on leave, a bug turns out to be in a different team's domain, or a fix requires different skills. Reassignment rules:
Reassignment requires a comment explaining why the bug is moving and what the new owner inherits
For S1/S2 bugs, reassignment must be agreed between the current owner and the new owner — no surprise reassignments on high-severity items
The new owner acknowledges receipt explicitly in the ticket ('I am taking ownership of this from [previous owner]')
Reassigning a bug does not reset its age or priority — the age clock does not restart when ownership changes
Handling the 'Nobody Owns This' Anti-Pattern

The most common triage failure is a bug that has been triaged but has no active work because the owner has not picked it up, has moved to other work, or the assignment was unclear. Detect and address this pattern:
Stale Bug Detection

Automated stale bug alerts remove the manual burden of scanning the backlog. The alert goes to the owner and their manager — enough visibility to prompt action without creating noise for the whole team.
Triage Metrics to Track
Triage process health is measured through a small set of leading and lagging indicators:
Metric
Target
What it indicates
Bug triage latency (filing to S/P assignment)
< 24h for all bugs
Daily triage running on cadence
Unowned S1/S2 bugs
0 at any point
Ownership rules enforced
P1 MTTR (mean time to resolve)
< 3 days
Engineering capacity adequate for P1 load
P2 sprint fit rate
> 80% of P2 bugs scheduled within 2 sprints
Backlog prioritization working
Weekly triage attendance rate
100% required attendees
Process buy-in; Safety rep attendance critical
Stale P1/P2 rate
Ownership and update cadence enforced
Priority change rate per week
< 15% of backlog
Indicates stable initial classification; high rate suggests triage quality issues
Triage for AI-Specific Bug Categories

Several GenAI-specific bug categories require triage rules that do not exist in standard software triage. These categories need explicit handling to avoid them being classified incorrectly or languishing in the wrong queue.
Model Regression Bugs

A model regression is a quality decline introduced by a model version change — the model produces worse outputs on a previously-working use case. Model regression bugs are common after model updates and require a specialized triage path because the fix may be a model rollback (fast) or a model re-fine-tune (slow).
Triage for a suspected model regression:
Confirm regression by comparing outputs on the same input across model versions — run 20 representative examples and compare quality scores or outputs
If regression confirmed: classify as S2 or S1 depending on whether the affected capability is core (S2) or safety-critical (S1)
Assign owner to ML platform team — not L2, not L3 platform engineering; the fix path is through the ML team
Flag whether rollback is available: if the prior model version is still deployed, an immediate rollback may be possible while the regression is investigated
Enterprise customers using the affected model version must be notified within 24 hours if the regression affects their core use case
Safety Filter False Positives

A safety filter false positive — the filter blocking content that is legitimate — is a bug with a counter-intuitive severity profile. High false positive rates drive users to find workarounds (prompt injection patterns, adversarial inputs) and erode trust. The severity depends on the false positive rate and the affected input category:
False positive pattern
Severity
Triage action
Filter blocks a common legitimate query type (> 5% of that category)
S2
Assign to Safety team; requires filter tuning or prompt adjustment
Filter blocks a specific phrase or topic legitimately used by enterprise customer
S2
Immediate: account manager + Safety team; may require allowlist
Filter blocks medical / legal / academic content for professional users
S2
Safety team review; may need domain-specific filter configuration
Isolated false positive on unusual edge case (< 0.1% of that category)
S4
Log; monitor for recurrence; no immediate action

Safety filter false positives must  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'not', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  be resolved by simply loosening the filter without Safety team involvement. Reducing false positives can increase false negatives (harmful content passing through), which is the worse outcome.
Hallucination Bugs from Specific Domains

Hallucination bugs from specific domains (medical, legal, financial, code) are triaged differently because the same pattern of hallucination may appear across many customers but only be noticed by domain experts.
Triage indicators specific to hallucination bugs:
Can the hallucination be reproduced deterministically or only with sampling? — non-deterministic hallucinations are harder to fix and severity may be lower if rate is low
Is there a retrieval-augmented context that would prevent the hallucination? — if yes, the fix may be a product-level RAG improvement, not a model-level fix
What is the risk of a user acting on the incorrect information? — a wrong historical date is S4; a wrong drug name in a medical context is S1
Has this hallucination pattern been seen before across multiple customers, or is it isolated? — clustered hallucinations suggest a systematic model gap
Integrating Triage with the Development Sprint
The triage process has no value if it does not feed into engineering work. The connection between bug triage and sprint planning is the critical handoff that turns triage decisions into actual fixes.
Bug Capacity in Sprint Planning

Allocate explicit capacity for bug fixes in each sprint. A common mistake is treating the sprint as 100% feature work and expecting bugs to fit in around the edges. This leads to P2 bugs sitting in the backlog sprint after sprint.
A reasonable starting allocation for a GenAI platform in production:

Sprint capacity allocation (example — adjust based on product maturity):

  New feature work:    50% of engineering capacity
  P1 bug fixes:        10% (reserved; drawn on as needed; unused capacity -> features)
  P2 bug fixes:        20% (scheduled in sprint planning based on weekly triage)
  P3 bug fixes:        10% (opportunistic; picked up when feature work unblocks)
  Tech debt:           10%

At early production (< 6 months in production):
  Feature work typically drops to 30-40% as bug load is higher.
  P1+P2 bug capacity rises to 35-40%.

At mature production (> 18 months):
  Feature work rises to 60-65% as bug load stabilizes.
  P1+P2 capacity can drop to 15-20%.
Bug Fix Pull into Sprint

At sprint planning, the weekly triage output provides the prioritized P2 bug list. The engineering team pulls from the top of this list until bug fix capacity is consumed. P2 bugs not pulled into the current sprint retain their P2 status and are re-evaluated at the next weekly triage — they do not automatically demote to P3 simply because they were not scheduled.

If the P2 backlog is consistently larger than the sprint can absorb (more P2 bugs than capacity for three or more consecutive sprints), this is a capacity signal: either engineering team size needs to increase, or the P2 classification bar is too low (bugs that should be P3 are being promoted).
Emergency Bug Fixes Outside the Sprint
P1 bugs that arrive mid-sprint require an emergency response outside the normal sprint process:
P1 bug is assigned immediately at daily triage
Engineering manager confirms that the P1 bug displaces planned sprint work — this decision is made explicitly, not implicitly
The displaced sprint work is logged and not silently dropped — it goes to the next sprint's P2 queue or is re-evaluated
If multiple P1 bugs arrive in the same sprint, engineering manager escalates to leadership: sprint commitments cannot be met and delivery plans need adjustment
Summary

Triage cadence and ownership rules are the operational backbone of bug management for GenAI platforms. The three-cadence model — daily for classification and S1/S2 status, weekly for backlog prioritization and sprint alignment, quarterly for icebox hygiene and trend analysis — ensures bugs are acted on at the right speed for their severity without overwhelming engineering capacity with process overhead. Ownership rules require every bug to have a single named owner, with explicit update cadences by priority level and automated stale detection for bugs that fall through the cracks. The weekly triage is non-negotiable in requiring AI Safety team attendance — safety-relevant bugs in the backlog need a safety expert confirming that the current state is acceptable. Priority changes are logged with rationale in the ticket, not communicated verbally.
Key takeaways:
Three cadences: daily (classify new bugs + S1/S2 status), weekly (backlog priority + sprint fit), quarterly (icebox decisions + trend analysis)
Every bug must have a single named owner — team or group assignments are not acceptable
Safety-flagged bugs are forwarded to the AI Safety team immediately, not at the next triage meeting
Safety team representative is a required attendee at weekly triage — safety bugs in the backlog need expert sign-off on timelines
Priority changes require written rationale in the ticket; undocumented priority changes cannot be explained to customers
Automated stale bug detection removes the manual burden of scanning for orphaned high-priority items
